region_of_origin: India (Coastal regions: Goa, Karnataka, Kerala, Maharashtra)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for semolina-crusted fried fish; closest parent 'fried fish food product' (FOODON_03302051) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Fish, fried) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code for semolina-coated fried fish exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for semolina-crusted fried fish as a food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Rava Fish consists of one to two pieces of semolina-crusted fried fish, weighing approximately 150 grams in total.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Fish and Seafood (Fried Fish, Shallow Fried)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN values for shallow-fried fish (generic white fish, semolina-coated) adapted for Rava Fish. Rava Fish is a coastal Indian preparation of fish marinated in spices and coated in semolina (rava/sooji) before shallow frying. Macronutrient profile is consistent with ICMR-NIN shallow-fried fish data adjusted for semolina coating contribution (~10–15 g carbohydrate per 100 g from coating). Values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Fish, mixed species, cooked, breaded and fried' (FDC ID 175168) for validation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 (Atkinson et al.) — Semolina-based foods and fried fish analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Rava Fish. GI estimated at 48 based on analogous foods from the University of Sydney GI Database: semolina (rava/sooji) has a reported GI of approximately 55 (Atkinson et al. 2021), and the protein and fat content of the fried fish significantly attenuates the glycemic response. A composite GI of 48 is defensible for a semolina-coated fried fish dish with ~12 g carbohydrate per 100 g, consistent with mixed protein-fat-carbohydrate fried fish preparations. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (48 × 12.0 × 1.5) / 100 = 8.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g) from salt in marinade and frying process
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one piece (~75 g) to keep sodium intake below 285 mg per serving, and request reduced salt in the marinade where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated glycemic index (~48) due to protein and fat moderating carbohydrate absorption from semolina coating
type2_diabetes.guidance: Rava Fish has a low estimated GI (~48) and moderate carbohydrate content (~12 g per 100 g), making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in a standard serving of 150 g alongside non-starchy vegetables.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (11 g per 100 g) with beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from fish, offset by frying oil contribution
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fish component provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids, but shallow frying adds oil; individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer a smaller portion (~100 g) and choose preparations using heart-healthy oils such as rice bran or canola oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rava Fish (also called Rava Fish Fry or Semolina Fried Fish) is a popular coastal Indian seafood dish in which fresh fish fillets or whole fish are marinated in a spiced paste of turmeric, red chilli powder, ginger-garlic paste, lemon juice, and salt, then coated in semolina (rava or sooji) and shallow-fried in oil until the exterior is golden, crisp, and fragrant. The dish originates from the coastal cuisines of Goa, Karnataka, Kerala, and Maharashtra, where abundant fresh fish such as pomfret, kingfish (surmai), mackerel (bangda), and seer fish are commonly used. The semolina coating creates a distinctively crunchy crust that contrasts with the moist, flaky, well-seasoned fish interior. Rava Fish is typically served as a starter or side dish alongside rice and curry, or as a standalone snack. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein food (~18.5 g protein per 100 g) with moderate fat (~11 g per 100 g), a meaningful source of vitamin B12, vitamin D, selenium, and omega-3 fatty acids from the fish. The carbohydrate content (~12 g per 100 g) comes primarily from the semolina coating. The estimated glycemic index is low (~48), making it a relatively diabetes-friendly protein option. Sodium content is moderate (~380 mg per 100 g) due to the spiced marinade, which warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. In Singapore, Rava Fish appears on Indian restaurant menus as a seafood appetiser, often priced as a premium dish reflecting the quality of fresh fish used.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Rava Fish?
Is Rava Fish good for diabetics?
What fish is used in Rava Fish Fry?
What is the difference between Rava Fish and regular fried fish?
Is Rava Fish high in protein?
Can I eat Rava Fish if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for Rava Fish as a named dish exists; values are adapted from ICMR-NIN shallow-fried fish data with semolina coating adjustment and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central fried fish data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using semolina and fried fish analogues. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation is methodologically sound and the nutrient profile is internally consistent.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods) shallow-fried fish data with an adjustment for semolina (rava) coating contribution to carbohydrate (~12 g per 100 g). Cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175168 (Fish, mixed species, cooked, breaded and fried). 2. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Rava Fish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for a home-style shallow-fried fish preparation using vegetable oil; no industrial trans fats are introduced. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value 4.5 estimated from fish lipid profile), polyunsaturated_fat_g (3.2 estimated). All -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated values where defensible from ICMR-NIN and USDA sources. 5. The cuisine_tag 'Butter Chicken' in the input source data appears to be a field survey platform tagging artefact and has been disregarded; Rava Fish is an Indian seafood dish unrelated to Butter Chicken. 6. Halal status is assumed true as the dish contains fish and plant-based ingredients with no pork or alcohol in the standard preparation; this may vary by restaurant. 7. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (48 × 12.0 × 1.5) / 100 = 8.64, rounded to 8.6.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full nutrient profile from ICMR-NIN adapted data cross-validated with USDA FDC ID 175168.
Estimated GI of 48 from University of Sydney GI Database analogous foods (semolina GI ~55, attenuated by protein and fat).
Added mandatory multilingual local_names for Indian cuisine in Singapore context: Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Populated aliases_by_language and verified full consistency with flat aliases array.
Disregarded field survey 'Butter Chicken' cuisine tag as platform artefact.
Set ontology match_type values exclusively from allowed set: exact, closest_parent, none.
Documented all adapted/estimated values in notes and nutrient_source.
